questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A paddle wheel is coupled to a mass of $12 \text{ kg}$ via fixed frictionless pulleys. The paddle is immersed in a liquid of heat capacity $4200 \text{ J K}^{-1}$ enclosed in an adiabatic container. Suppose that during a time interval, the $12 \text{ kg}$ block falls slowly through $70 \text{ cm}$. Calculate the work done on the liquid.
questions.solution:
The work done on the liquid by the paddle wheel is equal to the decrease in the potential energy of the falling mass. Since the block falls slowly, there is no change in its kinetic energy.

The work done is given by:

$W = mgh$

Given:
Mass of the block, $m = 12 \text{ kg}$
Distance fallen, $h = 70 \text{ cm} = 0.7 \text{ m}$
Acceleration due to gravity, $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$

Substituting the values:

$W = 12 \times 10 \times 0.7$

$W = 84 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $100 \text{ kg}$ block is started with a speed of $2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ on a long, rough belt maintained in a fixed horizontal position. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the belt is $0.20$. Consider the scenario from a frame of reference travelling at $2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ parallel to the initial velocity of the block. As seen from this frame, the block is gently placed on a moving belt, and in due time it begins moving with the belt at $2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. Calculate the increase in the kinetic energy of the block as it ceases slipping past the belt.
questions.solution:
Let the ground frame be $S$ and the moving frame be $S'$.

In the ground frame $S$, the initial velocity of the block is $v_{i} = 2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ and the belt is stationary ($v_{\text{belt}} = 0$).

The frame $S'$ is moving with a velocity $v_{F} = 2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ parallel to the block's initial velocity.

Initial velocity of the block in frame $S'$ is:
$u' = v_{i} - v_{F} = 2.0 - 2.0 = 0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

When the block ceases slipping, it moves together with the stationary belt. The final velocity of the block in the ground frame $S$ is $v_{f} = 0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

Final velocity of the block in frame $S'$ is:
$v' = v_{f} - v_{F} = 0 - 2.0 = -2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$

Initial kinetic energy of the block in frame $S'$:
$K_{i}' = \dfrac{1}{2} m (u')^2 = 0 \text{ J}$

Final kinetic energy of the block in frame $S'$:
$K_{f}' = \dfrac{1}{2} m (v')^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 100 \times (-2.0)^2 = 200 \text{ J}$

Increase in kinetic energy of the block as seen from the moving frame is:
$\Delta K' = K_{f}' - K_{i}' = 200 - 0 = 200 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Water at $15^\circ C$ is contained inside a closed, thermally insulated copper vessel. The temperature increases to $17^\circ C$ after the vessel is vigorously shaken for $15$ minutes. The mass of the water is $200 \text{ g}$ and the mass of the vessel is $100 \text{ g}$. The specific heat capacities of copper and water are $420 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$ and $4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$, respectively. Ignore any thermal expansion. Calculate the work that has been done on this system.
questions.solution:
According to the first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta U = Q + W$.

Since the vessel is thermally insulated, $Q = 0$, which gives $W = \Delta U$.

The change in internal energy of the system is the sum of the changes in internal energy of the water and the copper vessel:

$\Delta U = m_w c_w \Delta T + m_c c_c \Delta T$

Substituting the given values:

$m_w = 0.2 \text{ kg}$, $c_w = 4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$

$m_c = 0.1 \text{ kg}$, $c_c = 420 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$

$\Delta T = 17^{\circ}\text{C} - 15^{\circ}\text{C} = 2 \text{ K}$

$W = (0.2 \times 4200 + 0.1 \times 420) \times 2$

$W = (840 + 42) \times 2$

$W = 882 \times 2 = 1764 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Water at $15^\circ C$ is contained inside a closed, thermally insulated copper vessel. The temperature increases to $17^\circ C$ after the vessel is vigorously shaken for $15$ minutes. The mass of the water is $200 \text{ g}$ and the mass of the vessel is $100 \text{ g}$. The specific heat capacities of copper and water are $420 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$ and $4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$, respectively. Ignore any thermal expansion. Determine the amount of heat transferred to the liquid-vessel system.
questions.solution:
According to the first law of thermodynamics, the change in internal energy is given by $\Delta U = Q + W$, where $Q$ is the heat transferred to the system and $W$ is the work done on the system.

The problem states that the copper vessel is thermally insulated. This means that no heat can flow into or out of the system from the surroundings. Therefore, the heat transferred to the liquid-vessel system is $Q = 0$.

The increase in temperature from $15^\circ \text{C}$ to $17^\circ \text{C}$ is entirely due to the mechanical work done on the system by vigorous shaking. We can calculate the increase in internal energy (which equals the work done) as follows:

$\Delta U = (m_w c_w + m_c c_c) \Delta T$

$\Delta U = (0.2 \times 4200 + 0.1 \times 420) \times (17 - 15)$

$\Delta U = (840 + 42) \times 2 = 1764 \text{ J}$

Although the internal energy increases by $1764 \text{ J}$, this energy enters the system as work, not as heat. Thus, the heat transferred is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $100 \text{ kg}$ block is started with a speed of $2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ on a long, rough belt fixed in a horizontal position. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the belt is $0.20$. Consider the scenario from a reference frame moving at $2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ along the initial velocity of the block. As observed from this frame, the block is gently set on a moving belt and eventually starts moving with the belt at $2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. Determine the work done in this moving frame by the external force holding the belt.
questions.solution:
Let the initial velocity of the block be in the positive $x$-direction. The ground frame is $S$ and the moving frame is $S'$, which moves with velocity $V = 2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ in the positive $x$-direction.

In the frame $S'$, the initial velocity of the block is $v'_0 = 2.0 - 2.0 = 0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$. The belt moves with a constant velocity $v'_b = 0 - 2.0 = -2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$.

The block experiences a kinetic friction force $f_k = \mu_k m g$ in the negative $x$-direction (the direction of the belt's motion relative to the block). The acceleration of the block in $S'$ is $a' = -\dfrac{f_k}{m}$.

The time $t$ taken for the block to reach the velocity of the belt is obtained from $v'_b = v'_0 + a' t$, giving $-V = 0 - \dfrac{f_k}{m} t \Rightarrow t = \dfrac{m V}{f_k}$.

During this time $t$, the displacement of the belt in $S'$ is $d'_b = v'_b t = -V \left(\dfrac{m V}{f_k}\right) = -\dfrac{m V^2}{f_k}$.

To keep the belt fixed in the ground frame, an external force $F_{ext}$ must be applied to balance the friction force exerted by the block on the belt. By Newton's third law, the friction on the belt is in the positive $x$-direction, so $F_{ext} = -f_k$.

The work done by the external force in the moving frame $S'$ is $W'_{ext} = F_{ext} d'_b = (-f_k) \left(-\dfrac{m V^2}{f_k}\right) = m V^2$.

Substituting $m = 100 \text{ kg}$ and $V = 2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$:

$W'_{ext} = 100 \times (2.0)^2 = 400 \text{ J}$.

Answer: $400 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas undergoes a process where its pressure varies linearly with volume from $10 \text{ kPa}$, $200 \text{ cc}$ to $50 \text{ kPa}$, $50 \text{ cc}$. Assuming no heat is added to or extracted from the gas, find the change in the internal energy of the gas.
questions.solution:
Given initial state:
$P_1 = 10 \text{ kPa} = 10 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$
$V_1 = 200 \text{ cc} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$

Final state:
$P_2 = 50 \text{ kPa} = 50 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$
$V_2 = 50 \text{ cc} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$

Since the pressure varies linearly with volume, the process on the P-V diagram is a straight line. The work done by the gas is the area under the P-V graph, which forms a trapezium:
$W = \dfrac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)(V_2 - V_1)$

Substituting the values:
$W = \dfrac{1}{2}(10 \times 10^3 + 50 \times 10^3)(50 \times 10^{-6} - 200 \times 10^{-6})$
$W = \dfrac{1}{2}(60 \times 10^3)(-150 \times 10^{-6})$
$W = 30 \times 10^3 \times (-150 \times 10^{-6}) = -4.5 \text{ J}$

According to the first law of thermodynamics:
$\Delta Q = \Delta U + W$

Since no heat is added to or extracted from the gas, $\Delta Q = 0$.
$0 = \Delta U + (-4.5 \text{ J})$
$\Delta U = 4.5 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the change in the internal energy of a gas confined in a rigid container when $100 \text{ J}$ of heat is provided to it.
questions.solution:
For a rigid container, the volume of the gas remains constant, so $\Delta V = 0$.

The work done by the gas is $W = P\Delta V = 0$.

According to the first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta Q = \Delta U + W$.

Given that the heat provided to the gas is $\Delta Q = 100 \text{ J}$.

Substituting the values, $100 = \Delta U + 0$.

Therefore, the change in internal energy is $\Delta U = 100 \text{ J}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas is taken from an initial state $i$ to a final state $f$ such that the ratio of its pressure to its absolute temperature is maintained constant. Determine the work done by the gas.
questions.solution:
Given that the ratio of pressure to absolute temperature is constant:

$\dfrac{P}{T} = \text{constant}$

From the ideal gas equation, we have:

$PV = nRT$

$\Rightarrow \dfrac{P}{T} = \dfrac{nR}{V}$

Since $\dfrac{P}{T}$ is constant, $\dfrac{nR}{V}$ must also be constant. For a given amount of ideal gas, $n$ and $R$ are constants, which implies that the volume $V$ of the gas remains constant throughout the process.

Since the volume is constant ($dV = 0$), the process is isochoric.

The work done by the gas is given by:

$W = \int P \, dV = 0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a system undergoes the process $abc$, absorbing $80 \text{ J}$ of heat while performing $30 \text{ J}$ of work. If the system performs $10 \text{ J}$ of work during the process $adc$, calculate the amount of heat that flows into it during this process.
questions.solution:
According to the first law of thermodynamics:
$\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W$

For the process $abc$, the heat absorbed is $Q_{abc} = 80 \text{ J}$ and the work done is $W_{abc} = 30 \text{ J}$.
The change in internal energy is:
$\Delta U = Q_{abc} - W_{abc}$
$\Delta U = 80 - 30 = 50 \text{ J}$

Since internal energy is a state function, the change in internal energy $\Delta U$ depends only on the initial and final states. Thus, $\Delta U$ is the same for both paths $abc$ and $adc$.

For the process $adc$, the work done is $W_{adc} = 10 \text{ J}$.
The heat absorbed during this process is:
$Q_{adc} = \Delta U + W_{adc}$
$Q_{adc} = 50 + 10 = 60 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a gas can be taken from state $A$ to state $B$ via three different paths. Determine the work done by the gas in each of the three paths ($AB$, $ACB$, and $ADB$ respectively).
questions.solution:
From the given $V-p$ graph, the coordinates of the states are:
$p_A = 10 \text{ kPa}$, $V_A = 10 \text{ cc}$
$p_B = 30 \text{ kPa}$, $V_B = 25 \text{ cc}$
$p_C = 30 \text{ kPa}$, $V_C = 10 \text{ cc}$
$p_D = 10 \text{ kPa}$, $V_D = 25 \text{ cc}$

The work done by the gas is given by $W = \int p \, dV$, which is the area under the curve projected onto the volume ($V$) axis.

For path $AB$:
The work done is the area of the trapezium formed by the line $AB$ and the $V$-axis.
$W_{AB} = \dfrac{1}{2} (p_A + p_B) (V_B - V_A)$
$W_{AB} = \dfrac{1}{2} (10 + 30) \times 10^3 \times (25 - 10) \times 10^{-6}$
$W_{AB} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 40 \times 10^3 \times 15 \times 10^{-6} = 0.30 \text{ J}$

For path $ACB$:
The process $AC$ is isochoric ($V = \text{constant}$), so $W_{AC} = 0$.
The process $CB$ is isobaric ($p = \text{constant}$), so $W_{CB} = p_C (V_B - V_C)$.
$W_{CB} = 30 \times 10^3 \times (25 - 10) \times 10^{-6} = 30 \times 15 \times 10^{-3} = 0.45 \text{ J}$
$W_{ACB} = W_{AC} + W_{CB} = 0 + 0.45 = 0.45 \text{ J}$

For path $ADB$:
The process $AD$ is isobaric ($p = \text{constant}$), so $W_{AD} = p_A (V_D - V_A)$.
$W_{AD} = 10 \times 10^3 \times (25 - 10) \times 10^{-6} = 10 \times 15 \times 10^{-3} = 0.15 \text{ J}$
The process $DB$ is isochoric ($V = \text{constant}$), so $W_{DB} = 0$.
$W_{ADB} = W_{AD} + W_{DB} = 0.15 + 0 = 0.15 \text{ J}$

The work done in paths $AB$, $ACB$, and $ADB$ are $0.30 \text{ J}$, $0.45 \text{ J}$, and $0.15 \text{ J}$ respectively.

Answer: $0.30\text{ J}$, $0.45\text{ J}$, and $0.15\text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: To transition a system from state $A$ to state $B$ along the path $ACB$ as shown in the figure, $50 \text{ cal}$ of heat must be supplied. Determine the amount of heat that needs to be supplied to move the system from $A$ to $B$ via the path $ADB$.
questions.solution:
The work done along the path $ACB$ is the sum of the work done during the isobaric process $AC$ and the isochoric process $CB$.

$W_{ACB} = W_{AC} + W_{CB}$

$W_{AC} = p_A (V_C - V_A) = 50 \times 10^3 \times (400 - 200) \times 10^{-6} = 10 \text{ J}$

$W_{CB} = 0$ (since volume is constant)

$W_{ACB} = 10 \text{ J}$

The work done along the path $ADB$ is the sum of the work done during the isochoric process $AD$ and the isobaric process $DB$.

$W_{ADB} = W_{AD} + W_{DB}$

$W_{AD} = 0$ (since volume is constant)

$W_{DB} = p_D (V_B - V_D) = 155 \times 10^3 \times (400 - 200) \times 10^{-6} = 31 \text{ J}$

$W_{ADB} = 31 \text{ J}$

According to the first law of thermodynamics, the change in internal energy $\Delta U$ is path-independent.

$\Delta U = Q_{ACB} - W_{ACB} = Q_{ADB} - W_{ADB}$

$Q_{ADB} = Q_{ACB} + W_{ADB} - W_{ACB}$

Substituting the given values and the calculated work done:

$Q_{ADB} = 50 \text{ cal} + (31 \text{ J} - 10 \text{ J}) = 50 \text{ cal} + 21 \text{ J}$

Using the mechanical equivalent of heat $1 \text{ cal} = 4.2 \text{ J}$:

$21 \text{ J} = \dfrac{21}{4.2} \text{ cal} = 5 \text{ cal}$

$Q_{ADB} = 50 \text{ cal} + 5 \text{ cal} = 55 \text{ cal}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the heat absorbed by a system undergoing the cyclic process depicted in the figure.
questions.solution:
For a cyclic process, the change in internal energy is zero ($\Delta U = 0$).

According to the first law of thermodynamics, the heat absorbed is equal to the work done:

$Q = W$

The magnitude of work done in a cyclic process is given by the area enclosed by the cycle on the indicator diagram.

From the given $V-p$ graph, the semi-axes of the closed curve are:

$r_p = \dfrac{300 - 100}{2} = 100 \text{ kPa} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$

$r_V = \dfrac{300 - 100}{2} = 100 \text{ cc} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3 = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$

The area of the cycle is:

$\text{Area} = \pi \times r_p \times r_V$

$\text{Area} = \pi \times (10^5 \text{ Pa}) \times (10^{-4} \text{ m}^3)$

$\text{Area} = 10\pi \text{ J}$

Using $\pi \approx 3.14$, we get:

$|W| = 10 \times 3.14 = 31.4 \text{ J}$

Therefore, the magnitude of heat absorbed is $31.4 \text{ J}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas undergoes a cyclic process $ABCA$ as depicted in the figure. If $2.4 \text{ cal}$ of heat is supplied during this process, what is the value of $J$?
questions.solution:
For a cyclic process, the change in internal energy is zero ($\Delta U = 0$).

According to the first law of thermodynamics, the net heat supplied is equal to the net work done:
$Q = W$

The net work done $W$ is the area enclosed by the cycle on the $P-V$ diagram.

From the given graph, the cycle forms a right-angled triangle with vertices at $A(100 \text{ kPa}, 500 \text{ cc})$, $B(200 \text{ kPa}, 500 \text{ cc})$, and $C(100 \text{ kPa}, 700 \text{ cc})$.

The base of the triangle is $\Delta P = 200 \text{ kPa} - 100 \text{ kPa} = 100 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$.

The height of the triangle is $\Delta V = 700 \text{ cc} - 500 \text{ cc} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$.

The work done is the area of this triangle:
$W = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \Delta P \times \Delta V$
$W = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (100 \times 10^3) \times (200 \times 10^{-6}) = 10 \text{ J}$

The net heat supplied is given as $Q = 2.4 \text{ cal}$.

Using the relation $W = JQ$, where $J$ is the mechanical equivalent of heat:
$10 = J \times 2.4$
$J = \dfrac{10}{2.4} = \dfrac{100}{24} \approx 4.17 \text{ J/cal}$

Answer: $4.17 \text{ J/cal}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As illustrated in the figure, a substance undergoes the process $abc$. Determine the value of $J$ if $2625 \text{ cal}$ of heat is supplied to the system and the internal energy of the substance increases by $5000 \text{ J}$.
questions.solution:
From the given P-V diagram, the process $abc$ consists of an isobaric process $ab$ and an isochoric process $bc$.

The work done during the isobaric process $ab$ is:
$W_{ab} = P(V_b - V_a) = 200 \times 10^3 \times (0.05 - 0.02) = 6000 \text{ J}$

The work done during the isochoric process $bc$ is zero since the volume is constant:
$W_{bc} = 0$

Total work done by the system is:
$W = W_{ab} + W_{bc} = 6000 \text{ J}$

According to the first law of thermodynamics, the total heat supplied in Joules is:
$Q = \Delta U + W = 5000 \text{ J} + 6000 \text{ J} = 11000 \text{ J}$

Given that the heat supplied is $2625 \text{ cal}$, the mechanical equivalent of heat $J$ is:
$J = \dfrac{11000 \text{ J}}{2625 \text{ cal}} \approx 4.19 \text{ J/cal}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas undergoes a thermodynamic process along the path $AB$ as depicted in the figure. Determine the change in the internal energy of the system given that $70 \text{ cal}$ of heat is extracted from the gas during this process.
questions.solution:
From the given graph, the initial and final states of the gas are:
$P_A = 200 \text{ kPa}$, $V_A = 250 \text{ cc}$
$P_B = 500 \text{ kPa}$, $V_B = 100 \text{ cc}$

The work done by the gas during the linear process from A to B is given by the area under the P-V curve:
$W = \int P \, dV = \dfrac{1}{2}(P_A + P_B)(V_B - V_A)$
$W = \dfrac{1}{2}(200 + 500) \times 10^3 \times (100 - 250) \times 10^{-6} \text{ J}$
$W = \dfrac{1}{2}(700 \times 10^3) \times (-150 \times 10^{-6}) \text{ J} = -52.5 \text{ J}$

Since $70 \text{ cal}$ of heat is extracted from the gas, the heat added to the system is negative. Using the conversion $1 \text{ cal} \approx 4.2 \text{ J}$:
$Q = -70 \text{ cal} \approx -70 \times 4.2 \text{ J} = -294 \text{ J}$

According to the first law of thermodynamics, the change in internal energy is:
$\Delta U = Q - W$
$\Delta U = -294 \text{ J} - (-52.5 \text{ J}) = -241.5 \text{ J}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, the change in internal energy is approximately $-241 \text{ J}$.

Answer: $-241 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas has an internal energy given by the relation $U = 1.5 pV$. The gas expands from $100 \text{ cm}^3$ to $200 \text{ cm}^3$ against a constant pressure of $1.0 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$. Determine the heat absorbed by the gas during this process.
questions.solution:
The process is isobaric, so the pressure $p$ of the gas is constant and equal to the external pressure.

The work done by the gas during the expansion is given by $W = p \Delta V = p(V_2 - V_1)$.

Substituting the given values:

$W = 1.0 \times 10^5 \times (200 - 100) \times 10^{-6} = 1.0 \times 10^5 \times 10^{-4} = 10 \text{ J}$

The change in internal energy is $\Delta U = U_2 - U_1 = 1.5 p_2 V_2 - 1.5 p_1 V_1$.

Since the pressure is constant, $p_1 = p_2 = p$, giving $\Delta U = 1.5 p(V_2 - V_1) = 1.5 W$.

$\Delta U = 1.5 \times 10 = 15 \text{ J}$

According to the first law of thermodynamics, the heat absorbed by the gas is $\Delta Q = \Delta U + W$.

$\Delta Q = 15 + 10 = 25 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas is confined within a cylindrical vessel equipped with a frictionless piston. The gas is heated slowly for some time. Throughout the process, $10 \text{ J}$ of heat is added, and the piston moves outward by $10 \text{ cm}$. Determine the increase in the internal energy of the gas. The cylinder's cross-sectional area is $4 \text{ cm}^2$, and the atmospheric pressure is $100 \text{ kPa}$.
questions.solution:
The process is isobaric since the piston is frictionless and moves slowly, maintaining equilibrium with the atmospheric pressure.

The work done by the gas is given by $W = P \Delta V = P A d$.

Substituting the given values:

$W = (100 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}) \times (4 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2) \times (0.1 \text{ m}) = 4 \text{ J}$

According to the first law of thermodynamics:

$Q = \Delta U + W$

Given that the heat added is $Q = 10 \text{ J}$, the increase in internal energy $\Delta U$ is:

$\Delta U = Q - W = 10 \text{ J} - 4 \text{ J} = 6 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Through fixed frictionless pulleys, a paddle wheel is connected to a $12 \text{ kg}$ mass. The paddle is immersed in a liquid of heat capacity $4200 \text{ J K}^{-1}$ located in an adiabatic container. Consider a time interval in which the $12 \text{ kg}$ block drops slowly by $70 \text{ cm}$. Determine the rise in the temperature of the liquid, assuming the heat capacity of the container and the paddle is negligible.
questions.solution:
The loss in potential energy of the block is converted into the internal energy of the liquid. Since the block drops slowly, the change in its kinetic energy is negligible.

Work done by gravity on the block is:

$W = mgh$

Substituting the given values ($m = 12 \text{ kg}$, $g = 10 \text{ m s}^{-2}$, $h = 0.7 \text{ m}$):

$W = 12 \times 10 \times 0.7 = 84 \text{ J}$

This work done increases the temperature of the liquid. Let $C$ be the heat capacity of the liquid and $\Delta T$ be the rise in temperature:

$W = C \Delta T$

Given $C = 4200 \text{ J K}^{-1}$:

$84 = 4200 \times \Delta T$

$\Delta T = \dfrac{84}{4200} = 0.02 \text{ K}$

A temperature change of $0.02 \text{ K}$ is equivalent to a change of $0.02^\circ \text{C}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Water at $15^\circ C$ is contained inside a closed, thermally insulated copper vessel. The temperature increases to $17^\circ C$ after the vessel is vigorously shaken for $15$ minutes. The mass of the water is $200 \text{ g}$ and the mass of the vessel is $100 \text{ g}$. The specific heat capacities of copper and water are $420 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$ and $4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$, respectively. Ignore any thermal expansion. What is the increase in the internal energy of the system?
questions.solution:
The increase in the internal energy of the system is given by the total heat capacity of the system multiplied by the change in temperature.

The change in temperature is $\Delta T = 17^\circ \text{C} - 15^\circ \text{C} = 2 \text{ K}$.

The total heat capacity of the system is the sum of the heat capacities of the water and the copper vessel:

$C = m_w c_w + m_c c_c$

Substituting the given values:

$C = (0.2 \text{ kg}) \times (4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}) + (0.1 \text{ kg}) \times (420 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1})$

$C = 840 \text{ J K}^{-1} + 42 \text{ J K}^{-1} = 882 \text{ J K}^{-1}$

The increase in internal energy is:

$\Delta U = C \Delta T$

$\Delta U = 882 \times 2 = 1764 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $100 \text{ kg}$ block is given an initial speed of $2.0 \text{ m s}^{-1}$ on a long, rough belt kept fixed in a horizontal position. The coefficient of kinetic friction between the block and the belt is $0.20$. Determine the change in the internal energy of the block–belt system as the block comes to a stop on the belt.
questions.solution:
The initial kinetic energy of the block is given by $K_i = \dfrac{1}{2}mv^2$.

Substituting the given values, $K_i = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 100 \times (2.0)^2 = 200 \text{ J}$.

Since the block comes to a stop, its final kinetic energy is $K_f = 0$.

According to the law of conservation of energy for the isolated block-belt system, the loss in macroscopic mechanical energy is converted into the internal energy (thermal energy) of the system.

Therefore, the change in internal energy is $\Delta U = -\Delta K = -(K_f - K_i)$.

$\Delta U = -(0 - 200) = 200 \text{ J}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Initially, a gas is at a pressure of $100 \text{ kPa}$ with a volume of $2.0 \text{ m}^3$. While keeping the pressure constant, the volume is changed from $2.0 \text{ m}^3$ to $2.5 \text{ m}^3$. Subsequently, the volume is held constant and the pressure is increased from $100 \text{ kPa}$ to $200 \text{ kPa}$. Finally, the gas is returned to its initial state such that the pressure varies linearly with its volume. Determine whether heat is supplied to or extracted from the gas during the complete cycle.
questions.solution:
The cyclic process consists of three steps:

Isobaric expansion from state A $(2.0 \text{ m}^3, 100 \text{ kPa})$ to state B $(2.5 \text{ m}^3, 100 \text{ kPa})$.

Isochoric process from state B to state C $(2.5 \text{ m}^3, 200 \text{ kPa})$.

Linear return from state C to state A.

Plotting these states on a $P-V$ diagram forms a right-angled triangle. The sequence $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow A$ traces a counter-clockwise cycle.

The net work done in a counter-clockwise cycle on a $P-V$ diagram is negative ($W < 0$).

From the first law of thermodynamics for a complete cycle, $\Delta U = 0$, giving $Q = W$.

Since $W < 0$, $Q < 0$, indicating that net heat is extracted from the gas.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas sample of $2.0$ mol undergoes the cyclic process $ABCA$ as shown in the figure. A total of $1200 \text{ J}$ of heat is withdrawn from the sample during the cycle. Determine the work done by the gas during the part $BC$.
questions.solution:
In the given cyclic process $ABCA$, the graph is a $T-V$ (Temperature-Volume) diagram.

1. Process $C \rightarrow A$:
The line is vertical, which means the volume is constant ($V = \text{constant}$). This is an isochoric process.
Work done, $W_{CA} = 0$.

2. Process $A \rightarrow B$:
The line passes through the origin, which means $T \propto V$. From the ideal gas equation $PV = nRT$, this implies that the pressure $P$ is constant. This is an isobaric process.
The work done is given by:
$W_{AB} = P \Delta V = nR \Delta T = nR(T_B - T_A)$
From the graph, $T_A = 300 \text{ K}$. The point $B$ is at the same temperature level as $C$ to close the cycle at the given upper temperature, so $T_B = T_C = 500 \text{ K}$.
Given $n = 2.0 \text{ mol}$ and using $R = 8.3 \text{ J/(mol K)}$:
$W_{AB} = 2.0 \times 8.3 \times (500 - 300) = 2.0 \times 8.3 \times 200 = 3320 \text{ J}$

3. Cyclic Process $ABCA$:
For a complete cycle, the change in internal energy is zero ($\Delta U = 0$). According to the first law of thermodynamics:
$Q_{\text{cycle}} = W_{\text{cycle}}$
We are given that $1200 \text{ J}$ of heat is withdrawn from the sample, so $Q_{\text{cycle}} = -1200 \text{ J}$.
Therefore, the total work done in the cycle is $W_{\text{cycle}} = -1200 \text{ J}$.

The total work done is the sum of the work done in each process:
$W_{\text{cycle}} = W_{AB} + W_{BC} + W_{CA}$
$-1200 = 3320 + W_{BC} + 0$
$W_{BC} = -1200 - 3320 = -4520 \text{ J}$

Answer: $-4520 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Initially, a gas is at a pressure of $100 \text{ kPa}$ with a volume of $2.0 \text{ m}^3$. While keeping the pressure constant, the volume is changed from $2.0 \text{ m}^3$ to $2.5 \text{ m}^3$. Subsequently, the volume is held constant and the pressure is increased from $100 \text{ kPa}$ to $200 \text{ kPa}$. Finally, the gas is returned to its initial state such that the pressure varies linearly with its volume. Determine the amount of heat that was supplied or extracted during the complete cycle.
questions.solution:
The given thermodynamic cycle consists of three processes:

State 1 to State 2: Constant pressure expansion with $P_1 = 100 \text{ kPa}$, $V_1 = 2.0 \text{ m}^3$ to $V_2 = 2.5 \text{ m}^3$.

State 2 to State 3: Constant volume process with $V_2 = 2.5 \text{ m}^3$, $P_2 = 100 \text{ kPa}$ to $P_3 = 200 \text{ kPa}$.

State 3 to State 1: Linear return to the initial state.

These processes form a right-angled triangle on the $P-V$ diagram. For a complete cyclic process, the change in internal energy is zero ($\Delta U = 0$). According to the first law of thermodynamics, the net heat supplied or extracted is equal to the net work done, which corresponds to the area enclosed by the cycle on the $P-V$ diagram.

The magnitude of the work done is the area of the triangle:

$|W| = \dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$

$|W| = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (V_2 - V_1) \times (P_3 - P_2)$

$|W| = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (2.5 - 2.0) \text{ m}^3 \times (200 - 100) \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$

$|W| = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 0.5 \times 100 \times 10^3 \text{ J}$

$|W| = 25000 \text{ J}$

Since the cycle is counter-clockwise, the net work is negative, meaning $25000 \text{ J}$ of heat was extracted from the gas.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The figure displays the variation of the internal energy $U$ with the volume $V$ of $2.0 \text{ mol}$ of an ideal gas in a cyclic process $abcda$. The temperatures of the gas at $b$ and $c$ are $500 \text{ K}$ and $300 \text{ K}$ respectively. Determine the net heat absorbed by the gas during the process. (Take $R = 8.3 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$ and $\ln 2 = 0.693$)
questions.solution:
For an ideal gas, the internal energy $U$ depends only on its temperature $T$.

From the given $U-V$ graph, we can identify the thermodynamic processes:
Process $a \rightarrow b$: $U$ is constant, which implies $T$ is constant. This is an isothermal expansion at $T_a = T_b = 500 \text{ K}$.
Process $b \rightarrow c$: $V$ is constant. This is an isochoric process.
Process $c \rightarrow d$: $U$ is constant, which implies $T$ is constant. This is an isothermal compression at $T_c = T_d = 300 \text{ K}$.
Process $d \rightarrow a$: $V$ is constant. This is an isochoric process.

For a cyclic process, the net change in internal energy is zero ($\Delta U = 0$).
According to the first law of thermodynamics, the net heat absorbed is equal to the net work done:
$Q_{\text{net}} = W_{\text{net}} = W_{ab} + W_{bc} + W_{cd} + W_{da}$

Calculating the work done in each process:
$W_{ab} = nRT_b \ln\left(\dfrac{V_b}{V_a}\right) = nR(500) \ln\left(\dfrac{2V_0}{V_0}\right) = 500 nR \ln 2$
$W_{bc} = 0$ (since volume is constant)
$W_{cd} = nRT_c \ln\left(\dfrac{V_d}{V_c}\right) = nR(300) \ln\left(\dfrac{V_0}{2V_0}\right) = -300 nR \ln 2$
$W_{da} = 0$ (since volume is constant)

Net work done:
$W_{\text{net}} = 500 nR \ln 2 - 300 nR \ln 2 = 200 nR \ln 2$

Substituting the given values ($n = 2.0 \text{ mol}$, $R = 8.3 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$, $\ln 2 = 0.693$):
$Q_{\text{net}} = W_{\text{net}} = 200 \times 2.0 \times 8.3 \times 0.693$
$Q_{\text{net}} = 400 \times 8.3 \times 0.693$
$Q_{\text{net}} = 3320 \times 0.693 = 2300.76 \text{ J}$

Rounding to the nearest given option, the net heat absorbed is $2300 \text{ J}$.

Answer: $2300 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the increase in the internal energy of $10 \text{ g}$ of water as it is heated from $0^\circ\text{C}$ to $100^\circ\text{C}$ and subsequently transformed into steam at $100 \text{ kPa}$. The density of the steam is $0.6 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$. Assume the specific heat capacity of water is $4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ }^\circ\text{C}^{-1}$ and the latent heat of vaporization of water is $2.25 \times 10^6 \text{ J kg}^{-1}$.
questions.solution:
Mass of water, $m = 10 \text{ g} = 0.01 \text{ kg}$

Heat required to raise the temperature of water from $0^\circ\text{C}$ to $100^\circ\text{C}$ is given by:
$Q_1 = m c \Delta T = 0.01 \times 4200 \times 100 = 4200 \text{ J}$

Heat required to convert water at $100^\circ\text{C}$ into steam is given by:
$Q_2 = m L = 0.01 \times 2.25 \times 10^6 = 22500 \text{ J}$

Total heat supplied to the system is:
$Q = Q_1 + Q_2 = 4200 + 22500 = 26700 \text{ J}$

Work done by the system against the constant external pressure during vaporization is:
$W = P(V_{\text{steam}} - V_{\text{water}})$

Neglecting the initial volume of water, the volume of steam is:
$V_{\text{steam}} = \dfrac{m}{\rho_{\text{steam}}} = \dfrac{0.01}{0.6} = \dfrac{1}{60} \text{ m}^3$

Substituting the values to find the work done:
$W = 100 \times 10^3 \times \dfrac{1}{60} = 1666.67 \text{ J}$

Using the first law of thermodynamics, the increase in internal energy is:
$\Delta U = Q - W$
$\Delta U = 26700 - 1666.67 = 25033.33 \text{ J}$

$\Delta U \approx 2.50 \times 10^4 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the change in the internal energy of $2 \text{ kg}$ of water when it is heated from $0^\circ C$ to $4^\circ C$. The specific heat capacity of water is $4200 \text{ J kg}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}$, while its densities at $0^\circ C$ and $4^\circ C$ are $999.9 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$ and $1000 \text{ kg m}^{-3}$, respectively. The atmospheric pressure is $10^5 \text{ Pa}$.
questions.solution:
The heat supplied to the water is given by $\Delta Q = m c \Delta T$.

Substituting the given values:

$\Delta Q = 2 \times 4200 \times (4 - 0) = 33600 \text{ J}$

The change in volume of the water is $\Delta V = V_2 - V_1 = m \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho_2} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_1} \right)$.

Substituting the densities:

$\Delta V = 2 \left( \dfrac{1}{1000} - \dfrac{1}{999.9} \right) = 2 \left( \dfrac{999.9 - 1000}{1000 \times 999.9} \right) = \dfrac{-0.2}{999900} \approx -2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3$

The work done by the water against the atmospheric pressure is $\Delta W = P \Delta V$.

$\Delta W = 10^5 \times (-2 \times 10^{-7}) = -0.02 \text{ J}$

According to the first law of thermodynamics, $\Delta Q = \Delta U + \Delta W$.

$\Delta U = \Delta Q - \Delta W = 33600 - (-0.02) = 33600.02 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As depicted in the figure, a cylindrical tube of volume $V$ having adiabatic walls contains an ideal gas. The internal energy of this ideal gas is given by $1.5 nRT$. A fixed diathermic wall divides the tube into two equal parts. Initially, the pressure and temperature are $p_1, T_1$ on the left side and $p_2, T_2$ on the right side. The system is allowed sufficient time for the temperatures on both sides to become equal. Determine the work that has been done by the gas in the left part.
questions.solution:
The problem states that the diathermic wall dividing the cylindrical tube is fixed.

This means that the volume of both the left and right parts of the tube remains constant throughout the process.

The work done by a gas is given by the integral of pressure with respect to volume:

$W = \int p \, dV$

Since the volume of the left part is constant, the change in volume $dV = 0$.

Therefore, the work done by the gas in the left part is zero. The process is isochoric, and the heat exchanged only changes the internal energy of the gas.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical tube of volume $V$ having adiabatic walls contains an ideal gas. The internal energy of this ideal gas is given by $1.5 nRT$. A fixed diathermic wall divides the tube into two equal parts. Initially, the pressure and temperature are $p_1, T_1$ on the left side and $p_2, T_2$ on the right side. The system is allowed sufficient time for the temperatures on both sides to become equal. Find the final pressures on the left and right sides respectively. (Let $\lambda = p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1$)
questions.solution:
Let the volume of each part be $V_0 = \dfrac{V}{2}$.

The number of moles of gas in the left part is:
$n_1 = \dfrac{p_1 V_0}{R T_1}$

The number of moles of gas in the right part is:
$n_2 = \dfrac{p_2 V_0}{R T_2}$

Since the outer walls are adiabatic and the total volume is constant, the total internal energy of the system remains conserved. Let the final common temperature be $T_f$.

$U_{initial} = U_{final}$
$n_1 C_v T_1 + n_2 C_v T_2 = (n_1 + n_2) C_v T_f$

Solving for $T_f$:
$T_f = \dfrac{n_1 T_1 + n_2 T_2}{n_1 + n_2}$

Substituting the expressions for $n_1$ and $n_2$:
$n_1 T_1 + n_2 T_2 = \dfrac{p_1 V_0}{R} + \dfrac{p_2 V_0}{R} = \dfrac{V_0}{R}(p_1 + p_2)$

$n_1 + n_2 = \dfrac{V_0}{R} \left( \dfrac{p_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{p_2}{T_2} \right) = \dfrac{V_0}{R} \left( \dfrac{p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1}{T_1 T_2} \right)$

Therefore, the final temperature is:
$T_f = \dfrac{\dfrac{V_0}{R}(p_1 + p_2)}{\dfrac{V_0}{R} \left( \dfrac{p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1}{T_1 T_2} \right)} = \dfrac{(p_1 + p_2) T_1 T_2}{p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1}$

Given $\lambda = p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1$, we can write:
$T_f = \dfrac{(p_1 + p_2) T_1 T_2}{\lambda}$

Now, applying the ideal gas law to find the final pressure on the left side ($p_{1f}$) and right side ($p_{2f}$), keeping in mind that the volume of each part remains $V_0$:

For the left side:
$\dfrac{p_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{p_{1f}}{T_f} \Rightarrow p_{1f} = \dfrac{p_1}{T_1} T_f = \dfrac{p_1}{T_1} \left[ \dfrac{(p_1 + p_2) T_1 T_2}{\lambda} \right] = \dfrac{p_1 T_2 (p_1 + p_2)}{\lambda}$

For the right side:
$\dfrac{p_2}{T_2} = \dfrac{p_{2f}}{T_f} \Rightarrow p_{2f} = \dfrac{p_2}{T_2} T_f = \dfrac{p_2}{T_2} \left[ \dfrac{(p_1 + p_2) T_1 T_2}{\lambda} \right] = \dfrac{p_2 T_1 (p_1 + p_2)}{\lambda}$

Answer: $\dfrac{p_1 T_2 (p_1 + p_2)}{\lambda}$ and $\dfrac{p_2 T_1 (p_1 + p_2)}{\lambda}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas undergoes a process where its pressure varies linearly with volume from $10 \text{ kPa}$, $200 \text{ cc}$ to $50 \text{ kPa}$, $50 \text{ cc}$. Determine the work done by the gas.
questions.solution:
The work done by the gas in a process where pressure varies linearly with volume is given by the area under the P-V graph, which forms a trapezium.

Initial pressure $P_1 = 10 \text{ kPa} = 10 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$

Final pressure $P_2 = 50 \text{ kPa} = 50 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$

Initial volume $V_1 = 200 \text{ cc} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$

Final volume $V_2 = 50 \text{ cc} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$

The work done $W$ is calculated as:

$W = \dfrac{1}{2}(P_1 + P_2)(V_2 - V_1)$

$W = \dfrac{1}{2}(10 \times 10^3 + 50 \times 10^3)(50 \times 10^{-6} - 200 \times 10^{-6})$

$W = \dfrac{1}{2}(60 \times 10^3)(-150 \times 10^{-6})$

$W = 30 \times 10^3 \times (-150 \times 10^{-6})$

$W = -4500 \times 10^{-3} \text{ J}$

$W = -4.5 \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical tube of volume $V$ having adiabatic walls contains an ideal gas. The internal energy of this ideal gas is given by $1.5 nRT$. A fixed diathermic wall divides the tube into two equal parts. Initially, the pressure and temperature are $p_1, T_1$ on the left side and $p_2, T_2$ on the right side. The system is allowed sufficient time for the temperatures on both sides to become equal. Determine the final equilibrium temperature. (Let $\lambda = p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1$)
questions.solution:
Let the total volume of the cylindrical tube be $V$. Since the diathermic wall divides the tube into two equal parts, the volume of each part is $\dfrac{V}{2}$.

The number of moles of gas in the left and right parts initially are:
$n_1 = \dfrac{p_1 (V/2)}{R T_1}$
$n_2 = \dfrac{p_2 (V/2)}{R T_2}$

Since the outer walls are adiabatic and the inner wall is fixed, there is no heat exchange with the surroundings ($Q = 0$) and no work is done by the system ($W = 0$). Thus, the total internal energy of the system remains conserved.

Initial internal energy of the system:
$U_i = U_1 + U_2 = 1.5 n_1 R T_1 + 1.5 n_2 R T_2$
$U_i = 1.5 \left( \dfrac{p_1 V}{2 R T_1} \right) R T_1 + 1.5 \left( \dfrac{p_2 V}{2 R T_2} \right) R T_2$
$U_i = \dfrac{1.5 V}{2} (p_1 + p_2)$

Let the final equilibrium temperature be $T_f$. The final internal energy of the system is:
$U_f = 1.5 (n_1 + n_2) R T_f$
$U_f = 1.5 \left( \dfrac{p_1 V}{2 R T_1} + \dfrac{p_2 V}{2 R T_2} \right) R T_f$
$U_f = \dfrac{1.5 V}{2} \left( \dfrac{p_1}{T_1} + \dfrac{p_2}{T_2} \right) T_f$
$U_f = \dfrac{1.5 V}{2} \left( \dfrac{p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1}{T_1 T_2} \right) T_f$

Equating the initial and final internal energies ($U_i = U_f$):
$\dfrac{1.5 V}{2} (p_1 + p_2) = \dfrac{1.5 V}{2} \left( \dfrac{p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1}{T_1 T_2} \right) T_f$

$p_1 + p_2 = \left( \dfrac{p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1}{T_1 T_2} \right) T_f$

$T_f = \dfrac{T_1 T_2 (p_1 + p_2)}{p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1}$

Given $\lambda = p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1$, we get:
$T_f = \dfrac{T_1 T_2 (p_1 + p_2)}{\lambda}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An adiabatic vessel of total volume $V$ is partitioned into two equal parts by a fixed, conducting separator. The left part holds one mole of an ideal gas ($U = 1.5 nRT$) and the right part contains two moles of the same gas. Initially, the pressure on each side is $p$. The system is allowed to rest for sufficient time until a steady state is achieved. Find the temperature on the left and right sides in the beginning, respectively.
questions.solution:
Total volume of the vessel is $V$.

Since the vessel is partitioned into two equal parts, the volume of each part is $\dfrac{V}{2}$.

For the left part, using the ideal gas equation $pV = nRT$:

$p \left(\dfrac{V}{2}\right) = 1 \times R \times T_{L}$

$T_{L} = \dfrac{pV}{2R}$

For the right part, using the ideal gas equation $pV = nRT$:

$p \left(\dfrac{V}{2}\right) = 2 \times R \times T_{R}$

$T_{R} = \dfrac{pV}{4R}$

The initial temperatures on the left and right sides are $\dfrac{pV}{2R}$ and $\dfrac{pV}{4R}$ respectively.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical tube of volume $V$ having adiabatic walls contains an ideal gas. The internal energy of this ideal gas is given by $1.5 nRT$. A fixed diathermic wall divides the tube into two equal parts. Initially, the pressure and temperature are $p_1, T_1$ on the left side and $p_2, T_2$ on the right side. The system is allowed sufficient time for the temperatures on both sides to become equal. Calculate the amount of heat that has flown from the gas on the right to the gas on the left. (Let $\lambda = p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1$)
questions.solution:
Let the volume of each part be $V_1 = V_2 = \dfrac{V}{2}$.

The number of moles of gas in the left and right parts are respectively:
$n_1 = \dfrac{p_1 (V/2)}{R T_1} = \dfrac{p_1 V}{2 R T_1}$
$n_2 = \dfrac{p_2 (V/2)}{R T_2} = \dfrac{p_2 V}{2 R T_2}$

The internal energy of the ideal gas is given as $U = 1.5 nRT = \dfrac{3}{2} nRT$. This implies the molar heat capacity at constant volume is $C_v = \dfrac{3}{2} R$.

Since the outer walls are adiabatic and the total volume is fixed, the total internal energy of the system remains conserved.
$U_1 + U_2 = U_{1f} + U_{2f}$
$n_1 C_v T_1 + n_2 C_v T_2 = (n_1 + n_2) C_v T_f$

Substituting the values of $n_1$ and $n_2$:
$\dfrac{p_1 V}{2 R T_1} T_1 + \dfrac{p_2 V}{2 R T_2} T_2 = \left( \dfrac{p_1 V}{2 R T_1} + \dfrac{p_2 V}{2 R T_2} \right) T_f$
$p_1 + p_2 = \left( \dfrac{p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1}{T_1 T_2} \right) T_f$

Given $\lambda = p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1$, we can write:
$T_f = \dfrac{(p_1 + p_2) T_1 T_2}{\lambda}$

The heat flown from the right gas to the left gas is equal to the change in internal energy of the left gas (since the volume of each part is fixed, work done $W = 0$).
$\Delta Q = \Delta U_1 = n_1 C_v (T_f - T_1)$
$\Delta Q = \dfrac{p_1 V}{2 R T_1} \left( \dfrac{3}{2} R \right) (T_f - T_1) = \dfrac{3 p_1 V}{4 T_1} (T_f - T_1)$

Now, substituting the value of $T_f$:
$T_f - T_1 = \dfrac{(p_1 + p_2) T_1 T_2}{\lambda} - T_1 = T_1 \left( \dfrac{p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_2 - (p_1 T_2 + p_2 T_1)}{\lambda} \right)$
$T_f - T_1 = T_1 \left( \dfrac{p_2 T_2 - p_2 T_1}{\lambda} \right) = \dfrac{p_2 T_1 (T_2 - T_1)}{\lambda}$

Substituting this back into the equation for $\Delta Q$:
$\Delta Q = \dfrac{3 p_1 V}{4 T_1} \times \dfrac{p_2 T_1 (T_2 - T_1)}{\lambda} = \dfrac{3 p_1 p_2 (T_2 - T_1) V}{4 \lambda}$

Answer: $\dfrac{3 p_1 p_2 (T_2 - T_1) V}{4 \lambda}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An adiabatic vessel of total volume $V$ is partitioned into two equal parts by a fixed, conducting separator. The left part holds one mole of an ideal gas ($U = 1.5 nRT$) and the right part contains two moles of the same gas. Initially, the pressure on each side is $p$. The system is allowed to rest for sufficient time until a steady state is achieved. Find the final common temperature reached by the gases.
questions.solution:
Let the initial temperatures of the left and right parts be $T_L$ and $T_R$ respectively.

The volume of each part is $\dfrac{V}{2}$.

For the left part:

$p \left(\dfrac{V}{2}\right) = (1) R T_L \Rightarrow T_L = \dfrac{pV}{2R}$

For the right part:

$p \left(\dfrac{V}{2}\right) = (2) R T_R \Rightarrow T_R = \dfrac{pV}{4R}$

The internal energy of the gas is given by $U = 1.5 nRT = \dfrac{3}{2} nRT$.

The initial total internal energy of the system is:

$U_i = \dfrac{3}{2} (1) R T_L + \dfrac{3}{2} (2) R T_R$

$U_i = \dfrac{3}{2} R \left(\dfrac{pV}{2R}\right) + 3R \left(\dfrac{pV}{4R}\right)$

$U_i = \dfrac{3pV}{4} + \dfrac{3pV}{4} = \dfrac{3pV}{2}$

Let the final common temperature be $T_f$. The final total internal energy is:

$U_f = \dfrac{3}{2} (n_1 + n_2) R T_f = \dfrac{3}{2} (1 + 2) R T_f = \dfrac{9R T_f}{2}$

Since the vessel is adiabatic and the separator is fixed, no heat is exchanged with the surroundings and no work is done. Therefore, the total internal energy of the system is conserved.

$U_i = U_f$

$\dfrac{3pV}{2} = \dfrac{9R T_f}{2}$

$T_f = \dfrac{pV}{3R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An adiabatic vessel of total volume $V$ is partitioned into two equal parts by a fixed, conducting separator. The left part holds one mole of an ideal gas ($U = 1.5 nRT$) and the right part contains two moles of the same gas. Initially, the pressure on each side is $p$. The system is allowed to rest for sufficient time until a steady state is achieved. Determine the increase in the internal energy of the gas in the left part.
questions.solution:
Let the initial volume of each part be $\dfrac{V}{2}$.

The initial internal energy of a gas is given by $U = 1.5 nRT = 1.5 pV_{part}$.

For the left part:
$U_{L,i} = 1.5 p \left(\dfrac{V}{2}\right) = \dfrac{3pV}{4}$

For the right part:
$U_{R,i} = 1.5 p \left(\dfrac{V}{2}\right) = \dfrac{3pV}{4}$

Total initial internal energy of the system:
$U_{i} = U_{L,i} + U_{R,i} = \dfrac{3pV}{4} + \dfrac{3pV}{4} = \dfrac{3pV}{2}$

Since the vessel is adiabatic and the separator is fixed, the total internal energy of the system remains conserved. The conducting separator allows heat exchange until a common final temperature $T_f$ is reached.

Total final internal energy:
$U_{f} = 1.5 (n_L + n_R) R T_f = 1.5 (1 + 2) R T_f = 4.5 R T_f$

Equating initial and final internal energies:
$4.5 R T_f = \dfrac{3pV}{2} \Rightarrow R T_f = \dfrac{pV}{3}$

The final internal energy of the left part is:
$U_{L,f} = 1.5 n_L R T_f = 1.5 (1) \left(\dfrac{pV}{3}\right) = \dfrac{pV}{2}$

The increase in the internal energy of the gas in the left part is:
$\Delta U_L = U_{L,f} - U_{L,i} = \dfrac{pV}{2} - \dfrac{3pV}{4} = -\dfrac{pV}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An adiabatic vessel of total volume $V$ is partitioned into two equal parts by a fixed, conducting separator. The left part holds one mole of an ideal gas ($U = 1.5 nRT$) and the right part contains two moles of the same gas. Initially, the pressure on each side is $p$. The system is allowed to rest for sufficient time until a steady state is achieved. Determine the work done by the gas in the left part during the process.
questions.solution:
The vessel is partitioned by a fixed separator, which means the separator cannot move and the volume of each part remains constant throughout the process.

Since the volume of the left part does not change, the change in volume is $dV = 0$.

The work done by a gas is given by the integral of pressure with respect to volume:

$W = \int p \, dV$

Substituting $dV = 0$ into the expression for work done, we get:

$W = 0$

Thus, the work done by the gas in the left part during the process is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An adiabatic vessel of total volume $V$ is partitioned into two equal parts by a fixed, conducting separator. The left part holds one mole of an ideal gas ($U = 1.5 nRT$) and the right part contains two moles of the same gas. Initially, the pressure on each side is $p$. The system is allowed to rest for sufficient time until a steady state is achieved. Determine the heat given to the gas in the right part.
questions.solution:
Let the initial temperatures of the left and right parts be $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively.

Using the ideal gas equation $pV = nRT$:
For the left part: $p \left(\dfrac{V}{2}\right) = 1 \cdot R \cdot T_1 \Rightarrow T_1 = \dfrac{pV}{2R}$
For the right part: $p \left(\dfrac{V}{2}\right) = 2 \cdot R \cdot T_2 \Rightarrow T_2 = \dfrac{pV}{4R}$

Since the vessel is adiabatic, the total internal energy of the system remains constant. Let the final equilibrium temperature be $T_f$.

$n_1 C_v T_1 + n_2 C_v T_2 = (n_1 + n_2) C_v T_f$

$1 \cdot \dfrac{pV}{2R} + 2 \cdot \dfrac{pV}{4R} = (1 + 2) T_f$

$\dfrac{pV}{R} = 3 T_f \Rightarrow T_f = \dfrac{pV}{3R}$

The separator is fixed, so the volume of the right part is constant, which means the work done is zero ($W = 0$). By the first law of thermodynamics, the heat given to the gas in the right part is equal to its change in internal energy:

$Q = \Delta U_2 = n_2 C_v (T_f - T_2)$

Given $U = 1.5 nRT$, we have $C_v = 1.5 R = \dfrac{3}{2} R$.

$Q = 2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{3}{2} R\right) \left(\dfrac{pV}{3R} - \dfrac{pV}{4R}\right)$

$Q = 3R \left(\dfrac{4pV - 3pV}{12R}\right) = 3R \left(\dfrac{pV}{12R}\right) = \dfrac{pV}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As shown in the figure, a paddle wheel is linked to a mass of $12 \text{ kg}$ by fixed frictionless pulleys. The paddle is submerged in a liquid with a heat capacity of $4200 \text{ J K}^{-1}$, which is maintained inside an adiabatic container. Consider a time interval where the $12 \text{ kg}$ block descends slowly through $70 \text{ cm}$. What is the amount of heat given to the liquid?
questions.solution:
The container is given to be adiabatic. By definition, an adiabatic process is one in which there is no heat transfer between the system and its surroundings.

Therefore, the heat given to the liquid is $Q = 0 \text{ J}$.

The descending mass does mechanical work on the liquid ($W = mgh = 12 \times 10 \times 0.7 = 84 \text{ J}$), which increases its internal energy, but the heat transferred remains zero.
